enrichment.short_description: MockServer is a free, open-source Java-based tool that mocks and proxies HTTP, HTTPS, gRPC, WebSocket and TCP traffic so teams can test against realistic, controllable service behavior instead of fragile live dependencies.
enrichment.seo_title: MockServer Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MockServer review 2026: an open-source Java mocking and service virtualization tool for HTTP, gRPC and TCP testing. Features, setup, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MockServer is a free, open-source HTTP(S) mock server and proxy created by developer James D. Bloom and first released around 2013. It is written in Java and built on the Netty networking framework, and it is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license with no commercial company behind it, making it entirely free to self-host. The project lives on GitHub under the mock-server organization and has grown through community contributions to thousands of commits over its lifetime. Architecturally, MockServer can run as an embedded server inside a JVM test process, as a standalone Java server, or as a Docker container, and it exposes a REST API plus official client libraries for Java, JavaScript, Python, Ruby, Go, .NET, Rust and PHP, alongside a command-line interface. Its core function is to let developers and QA teams define expectations that return canned or templated responses for specific incoming requests, effectively simulating a dependency (a third-party API, a microservice, a payment gateway) that is unavailable, unstable, rate-limited, or expensive to call in a test environment. Beyond simple mocking, MockServer supports service virtualization use cases: recording and replaying real traffic to reproduce production behavior, verifying that expected requests were or were not made as part of test assertions, pausing and modifying live requests or responses with breakpoints, and injecting chaos such as latency, dropped connections, and malformed responses for resilience testing. It can template dynamic responses using Velocity, Mustache or JavaScript so a single mock can react differently depending on the incoming request. Newer releases have extended protocol coverage to HTTP/2, HTTP/3, WebSockets, gRPC, message brokers, and even AI/LLM API traffic, and the project has added a built-in Model Context Protocol (MCP) server so AI coding assistants such as Claude Code, Cursor and Windsurf can create expectations and inspect recorded traffic directly. MockServer's primary audience is backend and QA engineers building integration tests, contract tests, and CI pipelines for services with many external dependencies. Its closest competitors are WireMock (also Java-based and open source), Mockoon (a separate desktop-first mocking tool despite the similar-sounding name), Postman's mock servers, and Hoverfly. MockServer differentiates itself with very broad protocol support (TCP, gRPC, WebSockets and HTTP/3 in addition to standard HTTP), its embeddable JVM-native design for JUnit-driven testing, and, more recently, native MCP support for AI-assisted testing workflows. Because it has no managed SaaS tier, teams are responsible for hosting, scaling and securing their own MockServer instances, which is a tradeoff against fully hosted commercial mocking platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MockServer is a self-hosted, open-source framework for mocking and proxying the HTTP, HTTPS, gRPC, WebSocket and TCP services an application depends on. Rather than testing against real third-party APIs or unstable internal microservices, teams point their application at MockServer, which returns configured or templated responses on demand.

Created by developer James D. Bloom and written in Java on top of Netty, MockServer can run embedded inside a test process, as a standalone JVM server, or as a Docker container. It ships client libraries for many languages and a plain REST API, so it fits into almost any test stack regardless of the application's own language.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MockServer goes beyond static stubbing. It supports request verification as test assertions, recording and replaying real traffic to reproduce production scenarios, and breakpoints that let engineers pause and modify requests or responses mid-flight for debugging.

It also supports fault and chaos injection (latency, dropped connections, malformed responses) for resilience testing, dynamic response templating with Velocity, Mustache or JavaScript, and declarative load scenarios for basic performance testing. Recent versions add a built-in Model Context Protocol server so AI coding assistants can create and inspect expectations directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MockServer is completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no paid tier, usage limits, or SaaS account requirement. Teams self-host it via a JAR, embedded dependency, or Docker image, so the only real cost is the infrastructure used to run it.
Because there is no commercial vendor, there is no official support SLA; support comes from community channels, documentation, and the GitHub repository rather than a paid contract.

features.title: HTTP and HTTPS mocking
features.description: Define expectations that return specific responses for matching requests, simulating any REST or web API dependency.

features.title: Multi-protocol support
features.description: Mocks and proxies HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, gRPC, WebSockets and raw TCP traffic on a single port.

features.title: Record and replay
features.description: Captures real traffic to a target system and replays it later to reproduce production behavior in tests.

features.title: Request verification
features.description: Asserts that expected requests were or were not made, letting MockServer double as a test verification tool.

features.title: Fault and chaos injection
features.description: Introduces latency, errors, dropped connections and malformed responses to test resilience under failure.

features.title: Dynamic response templating
features.description: Generates responses that react to the incoming request using Velocity, Mustache or JavaScript templates.

features.title: Breakpoints
features.description: Pauses live traffic to inspect or modify a request or response before it continues, useful for debugging integrations.

features.title: Broad client and deployment support
features.description: Ships client libraries for Java, JavaScript, Python, Ruby, Go, .NET, Rust and PHP, plus Docker, CLI and JVM embedding options.

pros: Completely free and open source with no usage limits
Very broad protocol coverage including gRPC, WebSockets, TCP and HTTP/3
Embeds directly into JVM test suites for fast, isolated integration testing
Supports advanced service virtualization features like recording, replay and chaos injection
Active GitHub project with clients across many languages
cons: No managed hosted offering, so teams must run and maintain their own instance
Steeper learning curve than lightweight mocking tools for non-JVM teams
No official commercial support or SLA
Documentation can be dense for newcomers unfamiliar with expectation matching syntax
Less polished UI compared to some commercial API mocking platforms

faqs.question: Is MockServer free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. MockServer is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host, with no paid tiers or usage caps.

faqs.question: What is MockServer used for?
faqs.answer: It is used to mock and proxy HTTP, gRPC, WebSocket and TCP dependencies during integration testing, letting teams simulate third-party APIs or unstable services in a controlled way.

faqs.question: Is MockServer the same as Mockoon?
faqs.answer: No. MockServer and Mockoon are separate, unrelated projects despite the similar naming. MockServer is a Java-based server and proxy, while Mockoon is a desktop-first mock API tool.

faqs.question: Can MockServer mock gRPC and WebSocket traffic?
faqs.answer: Yes. In addition to standard HTTP and HTTPS, MockServer supports gRPC, WebSockets, TCP and, in newer versions, HTTP/3 and message brokers.

faqs.question: Does MockServer support Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes. MockServer publishes official Docker images, making it easy to run in CI pipelines or local development environments.

faqs.question: Who created MockServer?
faqs.answer: MockServer was created by developer James D. Bloom and has been maintained as an open-source project since around 2013.

use_cases.title: Integration test isolation
use_cases.description: QA and backend engineers mock third-party APIs and internal microservices so integration tests run reliably without depending on live systems.

use_cases.title: Resilience and chaos testing
use_cases.description: Teams inject latency, errors and dropped connections to verify how an application behaves when dependencies fail.

use_cases.title: Contract and CI testing
use_cases.description: Engineering teams embed MockServer inside CI pipelines to verify request/response contracts between services before deployment.

features: Full HTTP, HTTPS, gRPC, WebSocket and TCP mocking

enrichment.short_description: Modal is a serverless cloud platform that lets developers run Python-native AI, ML and data workloads, including GPU jobs, with per-second billing and no infrastructure management.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Modal Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Modal review 2026: the serverless GPU and CPU cloud for AI and ML workloads. Compare features, per-second pricing, and top Modal alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Modal is a serverless cloud compute platform built for running AI, machine learning, and data-intensive workloads, including GPU-accelerated jobs, without managing servers, containers, or Kubernetes clusters. The company, Modal Labs, Inc., was founded in January 2021 by Erik Bernhardsson, a former engineering leader at Spotify and Better.com and the creator of open-source tools Luigi and Annoy, alongside co-founder and CTO Akshat Bubna. Modal is headquartered in New York City with additional offices in San Francisco and Stockholm, and had roughly 100 to 150 employees as of 2026. The company raised an $87 million Series B in September 2025 led by Lux Capital with participation from existing investors Redpoint Ventures and Amplify Partners, pushing its valuation to about $1.1 billion and unicorn status. Architecturally, Modal's core innovation is a Python-native developer experience: instead of writing Dockerfiles and Kubernetes manifests, developers decorate ordinary Python functions (for example with @app.function()) and Modal handles containerization, scaling, and scheduling automatically, spinning up compute in seconds and scaling to zero when idle. Modal bills by the second for CPU, GPU and memory usage, which the company positions as the finest-grained billing in the serverless GPU category. Core features include serverless GPU functions supporting chips like A10G, A100 and H100, scheduled and event-triggered jobs, persistent storage volumes, web endpoint deployment for serving models and APIs, sandboxed code execution, and fast cold-start container images. Pricing is usage-based: a free Starter tier includes $30 per month in compute credits, three workspace seats, and modest concurrency limits; the Team plan costs $250 per month plus compute usage and includes $100 in monthly credits, unlimited seats, and higher concurrency; Enterprise pricing is custom. Illustrative per-hour GPU rates include A10G from about $1.10/hour, A100 from about $3.72/hour, and H100 from about $4.29/hour, though actual costs depend on per-second usage. Modal's target audience is AI/ML engineers, data scientists, and startups that need to run training, fine-tuning, batch inference, and data pipelines without dedicated infrastructure or DevOps teams. Its main competitors include Replicate, Banana, RunPod, Baseten, AWS SageMaker, and traditional Kubernetes-based ML platforms. Modal differentiates itself through its lightweight Python SDK, per-second billing granularity, and fast cold starts, aiming to remove the operational overhead typically associated with GPU infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Modal is a serverless cloud platform purpose-built for AI, machine learning, and data workloads. Instead of provisioning servers or managing Kubernetes, developers write plain Python functions and decorate them to run in the cloud, with Modal handling containerization, scaling, and GPU allocation automatically.

Founded in 2021 by Erik Bernhardsson and Akshat Bubna, Modal reached unicorn status after an $87 million Series B in September 2025 led by Lux Capital. The company is headquartered in New York with additional offices in San Francisco and Stockholm.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Modal's headline feature is serverless GPU compute with per-second billing, supporting GPUs like A10G, A100 and H100 alongside standard CPU functions. Containers spin up quickly and scale to zero automatically when not in use, so teams only pay for active compute time.

Beyond raw compute, Modal supports scheduled and event-triggered jobs, persistent storage volumes for datasets and model weights, one-line web endpoint deployment for serving models as APIs, and sandboxed execution environments, all driven from a Python-native SDK rather than YAML or Dockerfiles.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Modal uses usage-based, per-second billing for CPU, GPU and memory consumption. A free Starter tier provides $30 per month in compute credits along with a handful of workspace seats and modest concurrency limits, making it accessible for individual developers and small projects.

The Team plan costs $250 per month plus metered compute usage, and includes $100 in monthly credits, unlimited seats, and significantly higher container and GPU concurrency. Enterprise plans are custom-priced and add features like dedicated support and higher limits for larger organizations.

features.title: Serverless GPU compute
features.description: Run AI training, fine-tuning and inference jobs on A10G, A100 and H100 GPUs with per-second billing and automatic scaling.

features.title: Python-native SDK
features.description: Deploy functions to the cloud using ordinary Python decorators instead of Dockerfiles or Kubernetes manifests.

features.title: Fast cold starts and autoscaling
features.description: Containers start in seconds and automatically scale to zero when idle, minimizing wasted spend.

features.title: Web endpoint deployment
features.description: Turn Python functions into HTTP endpoints for serving models and APIs with minimal configuration.

features.title: Persistent storage volumes
features.description: Attach durable storage volumes to functions for datasets, checkpoints, and model weights across runs.

features.title: Scheduled and event-driven jobs
features.description: Trigger functions on a cron schedule or in response to events for batch processing and pipelines.

features.title: Sandboxed code execution
features.description: Run untrusted or dynamically generated code in isolated, secure containers, useful for AI agent workloads.

features.title: Per-second usage billing
features.description: Pay only for the exact compute time consumed, billed at second-level granularity across CPU and GPU resources.

pros: Python-native developer experience removes the need for Docker and Kubernetes expertise
Fine-grained per-second billing keeps costs closely tied to actual usage
Fast cold starts make serverless GPU workloads practical for interactive use cases
Free tier with monthly credits lowers the barrier for individual developers and small teams
Backed by well-known investors and a founding team with strong open-source and infrastructure credibility
cons: GPU costs can still add up quickly for sustained, high-volume training workloads
Primarily oriented toward Python; less native support for other languages
Team and Enterprise plans add a base subscription fee on top of usage costs
Newer platform compared to established cloud providers, with a smaller enterprise track record
Advanced networking and compliance features may lag larger hyperscalers

faqs.question: What is Modal used for?
faqs.answer: Modal is used to run AI, machine learning and data workloads, including GPU-accelerated training, fine-tuning, batch inference and data pipelines, without managing servers or Kubernetes.

faqs.question: How does Modal pricing work?
faqs.answer: Modal bills by the second for CPU, GPU and memory usage. It offers a free Starter tier with monthly compute credits, a $250 per month Team plan plus usage, and custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Does Modal support GPUs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Modal offers serverless access to GPUs including A10G, A100 and H100, billed per second based on actual usage.

faqs.question: Who founded Modal?
faqs.answer: Modal was founded in 2021 by Erik Bernhardsson, former engineering leader at Spotify, and co-founder and CTO Akshat Bubna.

faqs.question: Is Modal free to try?
faqs.answer: Yes. Modal's Starter plan is free and includes $30 per month in compute credits along with limited workspace seats and concurrency.

faqs.question: How much funding has Modal raised?
faqs.answer: Modal raised an $87 million Series B in September 2025 led by Lux Capital, reaching a valuation of about $1.1 billion.

use_cases.title: AI model training and fine-tuning
use_cases.description: ML teams run GPU-intensive training and fine-tuning jobs on demand without provisioning or managing dedicated GPU clusters.

use_cases.title: Serving inference APIs
use_cases.description: Developers deploy trained models as scalable web endpoints that automatically scale with request volume.

use_cases.title: Batch data processing
use_cases.description: Data teams schedule and run large-scale batch jobs and pipelines using serverless Python functions.

enrichment.short_description: Modoboa is an open-source mail hosting and administration platform that bundles Postfix and Dovecot with a modern web admin panel and webmail client.
enrichment.seo_title: Modoboa Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Modoboa review 2026: an open-source mail hosting and admin panel built on Postfix and Dovecot. Features, self-hosting setup, and pricing compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Modoboa is an open-source mail hosting and management platform created by French developer Antoine Nguyen. The project originally started under the name MailNG before being renamed Modoboa, a portmanteau of 'Modular domains and mailboxes hosting.' It is free software distributed under the ISC license, with all source code available on GitHub, and the community reports it powers over 800,000 mailboxes worldwide as of recent counts. Architecturally, Modoboa is written in Python 3 using the Django web framework for its backend and Vue.js for its frontend, and it acts as an administration layer over battle-tested mail server components: Postfix for SMTP, Dovecot for IMAP/POP3, and a SQL database (MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, or SQLite) as the central data store tying everything together. Rather than building a mail server from scratch, Modoboa orchestrates and configures these existing open-source components and exposes them through a simplified web interface, which is its core value proposition versus manually configuring Postfix and Dovecot by hand. Core features include a multi-domain, multi-mailbox administration panel; an integrated webmail client with calendar and address book functionality; Sieve-based mail filtering and auto-responders; security and reputation tooling including SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and DNSBL checks with TLS via Let's Encrypt; real-time statistics and monitoring; and a modular extension system, since all current Modoboa features are implemented as pluggable extensions. The platform targets self-hosters, small businesses, IT administrators, managed service providers, and hosting companies who want to run their own email infrastructure instead of relying on Google Workspace or Microsoft 365. Modoboa itself is entirely free and open source with no licensing fees, but the team behind it also offers Modoboa Pro, a commercial add-on suite and professional services (installation, upgrades, issue support, and annual maintenance) aimed at hosting providers, MSPs, and agencies running email at scale; professional services are priced individually, for example installation or upgrade assistance starting from roughly 150 euros. Modoboa's main competitors in the self-hosted mail space include Mail-in-a-Box, iRedMail, Mailcow, and Poste.io, as well as commercial alternatives like Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 for organizations that prefer not to self-host. Modoboa differentiates itself with its modern Django/Vue-based admin UI, modular extension architecture, and an active open-source community, while remaining fully self-hosted and free at its core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Modoboa is a free, open-source platform for hosting and administering email. It wraps proven mail server components, Postfix for sending mail and Dovecot for retrieving it, in a modern, easy-to-use web administration panel and webmail client.

Created by French developer Antoine Nguyen and originally released under the name MailNG, Modoboa is written in Python using Django and Vue.js, and stores its configuration in a SQL database such as MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, or SQLite. The community reports the platform now powers hundreds of thousands of mailboxes worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Modoboa provides multi-domain and multi-mailbox management, an integrated webmail client with calendar and address book, and Sieve-based filtering and auto-responders. Administrators get real-time statistics, monitoring, and permission management from a single panel.

On the security side, Modoboa supports SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and DNSBL checks alongside automatic TLS via Let's Encrypt, helping protect domain reputation and deliverability. Because all functionality is built as modular extensions, administrators can enable only the features they need.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Modoboa platform is entirely free and open source under the ISC license, with no cost to download, self-host, or use at any scale. Users are responsible for their own server infrastructure.

For organizations that want commercial support, the team also offers Modoboa Pro, a paid extension suite, along with professional services such as installation, upgrades, and issue troubleshooting, priced individually starting from roughly 150 euros for setup assistance.

features.title: Web-based admin panel
features.description: Centralized panel for managing domains, mailboxes, aliases, and permissions without touching Postfix or Dovecot configuration files directly.

features.title: Integrated webmail
features.description: Full-featured webmail client with calendar and address book built into the platform.

features.title: Multi-domain, unlimited mailbox support
features.description: Hosts unlimited domains, mailboxes, and aliases from a single Modoboa installation.

features.title: Sieve filters and auto-responders
features.description: Lets users and admins configure mail filtering rules and automatic reply messages.

features.title: Email authentication and reputation tools
features.description: Built-in support for SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and DNSBL checks to protect deliverability.

features.title: Automatic TLS via Let's Encrypt
features.description: Provisions and renews TLS certificates automatically to secure mail traffic.

features.title: Modular extension architecture
features.description: All features are implemented as extensions, letting administrators customize which capabilities are enabled.

features.title: Monitoring and statistics
features.description: Provides real-time statistics and monitoring dashboards for mail traffic and server health.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing costs
Modern Django and Vue-based admin interface, unusual for a self-hosted mail project
Built on proven, widely-used components (Postfix, Dovecot)
Strong built-in security and reputation tooling (SPF, DKIM, DMARC)
Active open-source community with hundreds of thousands of mailboxes deployed
cons: Requires self-hosting and server administration knowledge to deploy and maintain
No official managed cloud version directly from the core project
Commercial Pro features and professional support pricing are not fully transparent
Smaller ecosystem and plugin marketplace compared to commercial email platforms
Documentation is comprehensive but assumes familiarity with Linux mail server concepts

faqs.question: Is Modoboa free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Modoboa platform is free and open source under the ISC license. A paid Modoboa Pro extension suite and professional services are also available for organizations that want additional support.

faqs.question: What technology is Modoboa built on?
faqs.answer: Modoboa is written in Python 3 using the Django framework for the backend and Vue.js for the frontend, and it manages Postfix and Dovecot as the underlying mail server components.

faqs.question: Who created Modoboa?
faqs.answer: Modoboa was created by French developer Antoine Nguyen, originally under the project name MailNG before it was renamed.

faqs.question: Do I need server administration skills to use Modoboa?
faqs.answer: Yes. Modoboa is self-hosted software, so you need a Linux server and basic sysadmin knowledge to install and maintain it, though the web panel simplifies day-to-day administration.

faqs.question: Does Modoboa include webmail?
faqs.answer: Yes, Modoboa ships with an integrated webmail client that includes calendar and address book functionality.

faqs.question: How does Modoboa compare to Mailcow or iRedMail?
faqs.answer: Modoboa, Mailcow, and iRedMail are all open-source self-hosted mail platforms built around Postfix and Dovecot; they differ mainly in admin UI design, extension architecture, and deployment method (Modoboa favors a Django/Vue app, Mailcow favors Docker containers).

use_cases.title: Self-hosted email for small businesses
use_cases.description: Small businesses run their own domain email infrastructure instead of paying for Google Workspace or Microsoft 365 seats.

use_cases.title: Hosting provider mail platform
use_cases.description: Web hosts and MSPs use Modoboa to offer multi-tenant email hosting to their own customers.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused personal email hosting
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious individuals and developers self-host their own mail server to control their data.

pricing_plans.price: From approximately EUR 150 for setup assistance

enrichment.short_description: MODX is an open-source PHP content management system and framework that gives developers full control over markup to build custom, semantic websites without a fixed theme structure.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid MODX Cloud hosting from about $39/month)
enrichment.seo_title: MODX Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MODX CMS review 2026: an open-source, developer-first PHP content management framework. Features, MODX Cloud pricing, and top CMS alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MODX is an open-source content management system and framework built on PHP, created in 2004 by developers Raymond Irving and Ryan Thrash, with Jason Coward joining the project leadership team in 2005. The founders wanted a CMS that let them build websites with fully semantic HTML, styled purely with CSS, and no existing platform met that bar, so they built and open-sourced what became MODX Evolution. MODX LLC was formally established in May 2010, and later that year the team released MODX Revolution, a complete architectural rewrite of the original CMS that remains the actively developed branch today. The company is privately held and based in Dallas, Texas, with its original founders still guiding the project. In October 2012, MODX launched MODX Cloud, a managed hosting service built specifically for MODX sites. Architecturally, MODX distinguishes itself from theme-driven CMS platforms like WordPress by imposing no default templates or markup: every element of the front end is built from scratch using MODX's own tag-based templating (Chunks, Snippets, Templates, and Template Variables), giving developers precise control over HTML output, which the project calls its 'anti-bloat' or 'Creative Freedom' philosophy. It uses a resource-based content model rather than a rigid post/page hierarchy, making it flexible for both traditional websites and more complex content structures. Core features include a manager (admin) back end for content editing, a plugin and extra ecosystem for extending functionality, granular user and permission management, multi-language support, and strong SEO-friendly URL handling out of the box. Because MODX is open source, the core software is free to download and self-host on any PHP-compatible server; premium add-ons from community developers have existed since 2013. For teams that do not want to self-host, MODX offers managed MODX Cloud hosting with tiered pricing: a Base plan around $39 per month, a Pro plan around $99 per month, and a Business plan around $349 per month, with custom Enterprise plans starting around $4,000 per month on a 3-year commitment; optional add-ons include staging environments, CDN/WAF protection, and multi-datacenter failover. MODX's target audience is developers, agencies, and technically sophisticated teams building custom-designed marketing sites, brochure sites, and small-to-midsize business websites who want full control over markup rather than a page-builder experience. Its main competitors include WordPress, Craft CMS, ProcessWire, and other open-source PHP CMS platforms, as well as headless CMS options. MODX differentiates itself through its markup-agnostic templating, long operating history since 2004, and a managed cloud hosting arm that generates revenue alongside the free open-source core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MODX is an open-source content management system built in PHP that gives developers complete control over a site's HTML output rather than forcing a fixed theme structure. It was created in 2004 by Raymond Irving and Ryan Thrash, who wanted a CMS that could produce fully semantic, CSS-styled markup, and the project has been maintained continuously since.

MODX LLC was formally founded in Dallas, Texas in May 2010, the same year the team released MODX Revolution, a full rewrite that replaced the original MODX Evolution codebase and remains the current actively developed version.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MODX uses a tag-based templating system built from Chunks, Snippets, Templates, and Template Variables, giving developers precise, markup-agnostic control over front-end output instead of relying on pre-built themes. Its resource-based content model supports both simple brochure sites and more complex, custom content structures.

The platform includes a manager back end for editing content, a permissions and user-role system, multi-language support, SEO-friendly URLs by default, and an ecosystem of community-built extras and plugins for adding functionality without touching core code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core MODX software is free and open source, and can be downloaded and self-hosted on any PHP-compatible server at no cost. Premium add-ons built by community developers have been available since 2013 for teams that want extra functionality.

For teams that prefer managed hosting, MODX Cloud offers tiered plans starting around $39 per month for a Base plan, scaling to $99 per month for Pro and $349 per month for Business, with custom Enterprise pricing starting around $4,000 per month on longer commitments. Add-ons like staging environments, CDN/WAF protection, and failover are priced separately.

features.title: Markup-agnostic templating
features.description: Chunks, Snippets, Templates, and Template Variables give developers full control over HTML output with no forced theme structure.

features.title: Resource-based content model
features.description: Flexible content architecture that supports simple pages as well as complex, custom content structures beyond a typical post/page model.

features.title: Manager admin interface
features.description: A back-end panel for editing content, managing resources, and configuring site settings.

features.title: Granular permissions
features.description: Role- and user-based access control for managing who can edit or publish specific content.

features.title: SEO-friendly URLs
features.description: Clean, customizable URL structures supported out of the box without requiring additional plugins.

features.title: Extras and plugin ecosystem
features.description: Community-built extras extend MODX with additional functionality while keeping the core lightweight.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Built-in tools for managing multi-language and localized content within a single install.

features.title: MODX Cloud managed hosting
features.description: Optional managed hosting platform purpose-built for MODX sites, including staging, CDN/WAF, and failover add-ons.

pros: Complete control over HTML markup with no imposed theme structure
Free, open-source core with a long, stable operating history since 2004
Flexible resource-based content model suited to custom site architectures
Strong SEO-friendly URL handling and clean output by default
Optional managed MODX Cloud hosting purpose-built for the platform
cons: Steeper learning curve than theme-driven CMS platforms like WordPress
Smaller plugin and extras ecosystem compared to WordPress
Requires more developer involvement to build a site from scratch
Smaller community and slower feature cadence than larger CMS projects
MODX Cloud pricing is higher than many generic shared hosting options

faqs.question: Is MODX free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core MODX CMS is free and open source and can be self-hosted on any PHP-compatible server. Paid options include community-built premium add-ons and MODX Cloud managed hosting.

faqs.question: Who created MODX?
faqs.answer: MODX was created in 2004 by Raymond Irving and Ryan Thrash, with Jason Coward joining the project leadership in 2005. MODX LLC was formally founded in Dallas, Texas in 2010.

faqs.question: What is the difference between MODX Evolution and MODX Revolution?
faqs.answer: MODX Evolution was the original CMS released around 2005; MODX Revolution, released in 2010, was a complete architectural rewrite and is the actively developed version today.

faqs.question: How much does MODX Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: MODX Cloud managed hosting starts around $39 per month for a Base plan, with Pro around $99 per month and Business around $349 per month; custom Enterprise pricing starts around $4,000 per month.

faqs.question: Is MODX good for beginners?
faqs.answer: MODX is more developer-oriented than beginner-friendly CMS platforms like WordPress, since it requires building templates and markup from scratch rather than using pre-built themes.

faqs.question: How does MODX compare to WordPress?
faqs.answer: MODX gives developers full control over HTML markup with no default theme system, while WordPress relies on themes and a much larger plugin ecosystem; MODX is generally favored for fully custom-designed sites.

use_cases.title: Custom-designed agency websites
use_cases.description: Web agencies use MODX to build fully custom-branded client sites without fighting a theme system.

use_cases.title: Semantic, SEO-focused marketing sites

enrichment.short_description: MoEngage is an AI-powered customer engagement platform that orchestrates personalized cross-channel campaigns across email, push, SMS, WhatsApp, in-app, and web.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (dual HQ with Bengaluru, India)
enrichment.seo_title: MoEngage Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MoEngage review 2026: an AI-driven customer engagement platform for email, push, SMS and WhatsApp campaigns. Features, pricing tiers, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MoEngage is a customer engagement platform (CEP) that helps product, growth, and marketing teams orchestrate personalized, cross-channel campaigns across email, push notifications, SMS, WhatsApp Business API, in-app messages, and web push. The company was founded in 2014 in Bengaluru, India by Raviteja Dodda and Yashwanth Kumar, and has since grown into a dual-headquartered company with offices in San Francisco and Bengaluru, alongside additional regional offices. MoEngage has raised a total of roughly $458 million in funding across multiple rounds, with investors including A91 Partners, ChrysCapital, Dragon Funds, Mars Growth Capital, and Schroders, among others, and the company has reported hundreds of millions of dollars in annual revenue. Architecturally, MoEngage combines a customer data platform layer, which unifies behavioral, demographic, and transactional data into a single customer view, with a campaign orchestration engine and an AI layer branded Sherpa AI that powers send-time optimization, churn prediction, and real-time personalization decisions. The platform's segmentation engine lets marketers build audiences from live behavioral and demographic data, and its drag-and-drop journey and campaign builder supports complex, multi-step, multi-channel campaigns without requiring code. Core features include unlimited multi-channel messaging (email, SMS, push, in-app, WhatsApp, web push) on its higher tiers, AI-driven predictive analytics and churn prediction, customer journey analytics, real-time transactional alerts, A/B testing, and enterprise governance features such as role-based access, audit logs, and PII encryption. MoEngage uses a custom, quote-based pricing model driven primarily by Monthly Tracked Users (MTU) or Monthly Active Users (MAU) rather than a flat per-seat fee, and it offers two primary published tiers: Growth, aimed at rapidly scaling brands, reported starting around $4,999 to $5,999 per month depending on MAU volume; and Enterprise, aimed at large organizations with millions of users, reported starting around $19,999+ per month and adding full AI orchestration, dedicated SLAs, and compliance controls. Most MoEngage contracts carry a minimum one-year commitment, and add-ons like customer journey analytics and AI offer decisioning are sold separately. MoEngage's target audience spans consumer apps, e-commerce, fintech, media, and retail brands that need to run high-volume, personalized lifecycle marketing at scale, particularly in mobile-first markets. Its main competitors include Braze, Clevertap, Iterable, WebEngage, and Netcore, with MoEngage often positioned as a strong option for teams prioritizing AI-driven personalization and value in emerging markets like India and Southeast Asia alongside its US and European presence.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MoEngage is a customer engagement platform that helps marketing and growth teams run personalized campaigns across email, push notifications, SMS, WhatsApp, in-app messages, and web push from a single system. It combines customer data unification, audience segmentation, and cross-channel orchestration in one platform.

Founded in 2014 in Bengaluru by Raviteja Dodda and Yashwanth Kumar, MoEngage has grown into a dual-headquartered company with hubs in San Francisco and Bengaluru, backed by roughly $458 million in total funding from investors including A91 Partners and ChrysCapital.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MoEngage's AI layer, branded Sherpa AI, powers send-time optimization, churn prediction, and real-time personalization decisions across channels. Its segmentation engine builds live audiences from behavioral and demographic data, and its drag-and-drop journey builder supports complex multi-step campaigns without coding.

The platform also offers customer journey analytics, A/B testing, real-time transactional alerts, and enterprise governance features such as role-based access controls, audit logs, and PII encryption for regulated industries like fintech and healthcare adjacent brands.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MoEngage prices custom contracts based primarily on Monthly Tracked Users (MTU) rather than a flat platform fee. Its two published tiers are Growth and Enterprise, both requiring a quote and typically a minimum one-year commitment.

The Growth plan is reported to start around $4,999 to $5,999 per month depending on monthly active user volume and includes unlimited messaging across supported channels. The Enterprise plan is reported to start around $19,999 or more per month and adds full AI orchestration, dedicated SLAs, and advanced compliance and security controls.

features.description: Triggers timely, event-driven messages such as order confirmations or fraud alerts.

Unified platform covering nearly every major engagement channel including WhatsApp
Powerful behavioral segmentation and journey orchestration tools
Well suited to high-volume, mobile-first consumer apps and e-commerce brands
Enterprise-grade governance, security, and compliance controls available
cons: Custom, quote-based pricing makes upfront cost comparison difficult
Minimum one-year contract commitments reduce flexibility for smaller teams
Can be expensive for lower-traffic apps given MTU-based pricing

Steeper onboarding and setup complexity compared to simpler email marketing tools

faqs.question: How much does MoEngage cost?
faqs.answer: MoEngage uses custom, quote-based pricing tied to Monthly Tracked Users. Reports indicate the Growth plan starts around $4,999 to $5,999 per month and Enterprise starts around $19,999 or more per month.

faqs.question: Who founded MoEngage?
faqs.answer: MoEngage was founded in 2014 in Bengaluru, India by Raviteja Dodda and Yashwanth Kumar.

faqs.question: Does MoEngage support WhatsApp marketing?
faqs.answer: Yes, MoEngage supports WhatsApp Business API messaging as one of its core engagement channels alongside email, SMS, push, and in-app messaging.

faqs.question: What is Sherpa AI in MoEngage?
faqs.answer: Sherpa AI is MoEngage's built-in AI engine that powers send-time optimization, churn prediction, and real-time content and channel personalization.

faqs.question: How does MoEngage compare to Braze or Clevertap?
faqs.answer: MoEngage, Braze, and Clevertap are all cross-channel customer engagement platforms; MoEngage is often noted for competitive pricing and strong traction in mobile-first markets like India and Southeast Asia alongside its US presence.

enrichment.short_description: Mollie is a European payment service provider that lets businesses accept online and in-person payments across dozens of methods with transparent, pay-per-transaction pricing.
enrichment.starting_price: Free to start; transaction fees from around 0.25 EUR + 1.8% for card payments
enrichment.seo_title: Mollie Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mollie review 2026: a European payment service provider with pay-per-transaction pricing and no monthly fees. Features, fees, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mollie is a payment service provider founded in Amsterdam in 2004, licensed as a payment institution by De Nederlandsche Bank (the Dutch central bank), and now serving more than 200,000 businesses across Europe. The company has raised over $1 billion in total funding, including an $800 million Series C round in 2024 led by Blackstone Growth, which valued Mollie at approximately $6.5 billion. Mollie is headquartered in Amsterdam, with additional offices across Europe including Ghent, Lisbon, London, Munich, Berlin, and Paris, and employs over 1,000 people. Architecturally, Mollie provides a unified payments API and dashboard that lets merchants accept dozens of European and international payment methods (Visa, Mastercard, iDEAL, Wero, SEPA Direct Debit, SEPA Bank Transfer, PayPal, Klarna, Riverty, Apple Pay, and Google Pay, among others) without needing separate integrations for each. The platform covers both online e-commerce payments and in-person point-of-sale payments, and it exposes developer-friendly SDKs and plugins for major e-commerce platforms like Shopify, WooCommerce, and Magento, positioning itself around fast integration (the company advertises roughly five-minute onboarding) and no lock-in contracts. Core features include a unified checkout supporting dozens of local and international payment methods, subscription and recurring billing tools, fraud prevention and risk management, multi-currency settlement, detailed transaction reporting and reconciliation, and marketplace/platform payment splitting for multi-vendor businesses. Mollie's pricing model is transparent, pay-per-transaction pricing with no monthly account fees for standard online payments: card payments from consumers in the European Economic Area start around 0.25 EUR plus 1.8%, with higher rates for commercial cards (about 2.9% plus 0.25 EUR) and non-EEA cards (about 3.25% plus 0.25 EUR); iDEAL and Wero cost a flat fee (around 0.32 EUR in the Netherlands, 0.39 EUR in Belgium); SEPA Direct Debit costs around 0.35 EUR; and PayPal costs PayPal's own rate plus 0.10 EUR. For in-person payments, Mollie offers a pay-as-you-go tier with no monthly fee and a Pro plan at 20 EUR per month with reduced transaction rates and a one-year commitment. Businesses processing over 100,000 EUR per month qualify for custom volume discounts, and eligible startups can receive waived fees up to 1,000,000 EUR in processed volume. Mollie's target audience ranges from small online merchants and startups to large multi-channel and marketplace businesses across Europe. Its main competitors include Adyen, Stripe, Checkout.com, and PayPal, with Mollie differentiating itself on transparent per-transaction pricing (no monthly account fees for the base plan), strong coverage of local European payment methods like iDEAL, and a self-service, no-lock-in onboarding experience aimed at SMBs as well as larger enterprises.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mollie is a European payment service provider that lets businesses accept online and in-person payments through a single API and dashboard, covering dozens of payment methods across the region. Founded in Amsterdam in 2004, Mollie is licensed by De Nederlandsche Bank and now serves more than 200,000 businesses.

The company has raised over $1 billion in funding, including an $800 million Series C in 2024 led by Blackstone Growth at a roughly $6.5 billion valuation, and now employs over 1,000 people across offices throughout Europe.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mollie's core product is a unified checkout that supports major card networks alongside local European methods like iDEAL, SEPA Direct Debit, SEPA Bank Transfer, Klarna, Apple Pay, and Google Pay, all through a single integration. It offers plugins for common e-commerce platforms like Shopify, WooCommerce, and Magento, along with a developer-friendly API.

Beyond checkout, Mollie supports subscription and recurring billing, fraud prevention, multi-currency settlement, detailed reporting and reconciliation, and payment splitting for marketplaces and platforms with multiple vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mollie uses transparent, pay-per-transaction pricing with no monthly account fees for standard online payments and no lock-in contracts. Card payments from EEA consumers start around 0.25 EUR plus 1.8%, while iDEAL and SEPA Direct Debit carry their own flat or blended rates.

For in-person payments, Mollie offers a free pay-as-you-go tier alongside a 20 EUR per month Pro plan with lower transaction rates and a one-year commitment. Larger businesses processing over 100,000 EUR monthly can access custom volume pricing, and qualifying startups may get waived fees on early processing volume.

features.title: Fraud prevention and risk management
features.description: Built-in fraud detection and risk tools to reduce chargebacks and fraudulent transactions.

features.title: Marketplace payment splitting
features.description: Supports splitting payments across multiple vendors for marketplace and platform business models.

features.title: E-commerce platform plugins

features.title: Transaction reporting and reconciliation
features.description: Detailed dashboards and exportable reports for financial reconciliation and accounting.

pros: Transparent, pay-per-transaction pricing with no monthly fees on the base online plan
Broad support for local European payment methods that boost conversion, like iDEAL
No lock-in contracts and fast onboarding for new merchants
Covers both online and in-person payments from one platform
Backed by strong funding and a large customer base across Europe
cons: Primarily focused on Europe, with less depth in some non-European markets compared to Stripe or Adyen
Transaction fees vary meaningfully by payment method and card type, which can complicate cost forecasting
In-person Pro plan requires a one-year commitment
Non-EEA card transactions carry noticeably higher fees
Some advanced enterprise features require custom/volume pricing negotiations

faqs.question: What is Mollie used for?
faqs.answer: Mollie is used by businesses to accept online and in-person payments across dozens of European and international payment methods through a single API and dashboard.

faqs.question: How much does Mollie charge per transaction?
faqs.answer: Mollie charges pay-per-transaction fees that vary by method, for example around 0.25 EUR plus 1.8% for EEA consumer card payments and a flat fee for iDEAL, with no monthly account fee on the standard online plan.

faqs.question: Is Mollie only for European businesses?
faqs.answer: Mollie is headquartered in Amsterdam and focuses primarily on European payment methods and merchants, though it also supports international card payments and cross-border transactions.

faqs.question: When was Mollie founded?
faqs.answer: Mollie was founded in Amsterdam in 2004 and is licensed as a payment institution by De Nederlandsche Bank, the Dutch central bank.

faqs.question: Does Mollie support subscription billing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mollie provides tools for recurring and subscription billing in addition to one-off payments.

faqs.question: How does Mollie compare to Stripe or Adyen?
faqs.answer: Mollie, Stripe, and Adyen are all payment service providers; Mollie is particularly strong for European local payment methods like iDEAL and markets itself on transparent per-transaction pricing with no monthly fees, while Stripe and Adyen offer broader global reach and more extensive platform tooling.

enrichment.short_description: Monday Sales CRM is a customizable, no-code CRM product built on the monday.com Work OS that helps sales teams manage pipelines, contacts, and post-sales workflows.
enrichment.seo_title: Monday Sales CRM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Monday Sales CRM review 2026: a customizable, no-code CRM built on monday.com. Pipeline features, pricing tiers, and comparisons to top CRM alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Monday Sales CRM is a purpose-built customer relationship management product from monday.com Ltd (formerly dapulse Labs), the same company that operates the broader monday.com Work OS platform. monday.com was founded in Tel Aviv in 2012 by Roy Mann and Eran Zinman, renamed from dapulse to monday.com in December 2017, and went public on Nasdaq in June 2021, raising roughly $235 million across its funding history before the IPO. Unlike general project-management boards, Monday Sales CRM is a dedicated, pre-configured template and product line layered on top of monday.com's underlying Work OS architecture, purpose-built for sales teams rather than general work management. It gives sales teams a visual, no-code CRM covering the full deal lifecycle: customizable deal pipelines, contact and account records, lead capture forms, and post-sale account and project management, all built from the same flexible board-and-column engine that powers monday.com's other products. Because it inherits the Work OS foundation, Monday Sales CRM supports the same automation engine, integration marketplace, and dashboard/reporting tools as monday.com, but its templates, fields, and AI features are pre-tuned for sales workflows specifically. Core features include two-way email sync with Gmail and Outlook, email templates and sequences for outreach, mass email sending, AI-powered email generation and lead data enrichment, no-code workflow automations, visual sales dashboards and forecasting, built-in quote and invoice generation, customizable lead capture forms, and over 150 industry-specific templates spanning sales, marketing, and related use cases. Monday Sales CRM is priced on a seat-based subscription model separate from (though similarly structured to) monday.com's Work Management pricing: Basic CRM starts around $12 per seat per month billed annually, Standard around $17 per seat per month, Pro around $28 per seat per month, and Enterprise is custom-quoted; annual billing saves roughly 18% versus monthly, and all paid plans require a minimum of three seats. A 14-day free trial is available on paid plans. Monday Sales CRM's target audience is sales teams and revenue operations functions at small-to-midsize businesses and departments within larger enterprises who want a highly visual, flexible CRM without heavy implementation overhead. Its main competitors include HubSpot CRM, Pipedrive, Salesforce Sales Cloud, and Zoho CRM. Monday Sales CRM differentiates itself through the same visual, drag-and-drop customization and no-code automation that defines the broader monday.com platform, letting sales teams reshape pipelines and fields freely rather than adapting to a rigid, pre-built CRM data model, though this flexibility can mean more setup work than opinionated, sales-only CRMs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Monday Sales CRM is a dedicated CRM product built by monday.com Ltd on top of its broader Work OS platform, designed specifically for sales teams managing pipelines, contacts, and post-sale relationships. It differs from general-purpose monday.com boards by shipping pre-configured CRM templates, fields, and AI tools tuned for sales workflows out of the box.

monday.com itself was founded in Tel Aviv in 2012 by Roy Mann and Eran Zinman under the name dapulse, rebranded in 2017, and went public on Nasdaq in 2021. Monday Sales CRM launched as one of several standalone products (alongside Work Management, monday dev, and monday service) built on the same underlying visual, no-code platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Monday Sales CRM includes customizable deal pipelines, contact and account records, and lead capture forms for managing the full sales lifecycle in one place. Two-way email sync with Gmail and Outlook, templated email sequences, and mass email tools support outreach directly from the CRM.

AI features assist with email generation and lead data enrichment, while no-code automations handle repetitive tasks like stage updates and follow-up reminders. Visual dashboards support sales forecasting, and built-in quote and invoice tools extend the CRM into post-sale account and project management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Monday Sales CRM uses seat-based subscription pricing with a minimum of three seats. Annual billing tiers run from Basic at around $12 per seat per month, Standard at around $17, and Pro at around $28, with Enterprise available on custom quotes.

Choosing annual billing saves roughly 18% compared to paying monthly, and monday.com offers a 14-day free trial on paid CRM plans so sales teams can evaluate pipeline and automation features before committing.

Inherits monday.com's powerful no-code automation engine and integrations
Combines pre-sales pipeline management with post-sales account and project tracking
AI-assisted email generation and lead enrichment built into paid plans
Large template library speeds up setup for common sales workflows
cons: Three-seat minimum can feel expensive for very small sales teams
Key CRM features are locked behind higher-priced tiers
Flexibility means more setup effort compared to opinionated, sales-only CRMs
Reporting and forecasting are less specialized than dedicated sales-analytics CRMs
Pricing structure has changed multiple times, making cost comparisons across years inconsistent

faqs.question: What is Monday Sales CRM?
faqs.answer: Monday Sales CRM is a purpose-built CRM product from monday.com, built on the same Work OS platform as the company's general project-management tool, but pre-configured for sales pipeline, contact, and post-sale management.

faqs.question: How much does Monday Sales CRM cost?
faqs.answer: Monday Sales CRM starts around $12 per seat per month for the Basic tier billed annually, with Standard around $17, Pro around $28, and custom Enterprise pricing, subject to a three-seat minimum.

faqs.question: Is Monday Sales CRM the same as monday.com?
faqs.answer: No. Monday Sales CRM is a specific CRM product built on the broader monday.com Work OS platform, while monday.com itself is a general-purpose work management and project tracking tool.

faqs.question: Does Monday Sales CRM integrate with email?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers two-way email sync with Gmail and Outlook, along with email templates, sequences, and mass email sending.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Monday Sales CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, monday.com offers a 14-day free trial on Monday Sales CRM paid plans.

faqs.question: How does Monday Sales CRM compare to HubSpot or Pipedrive?
faqs.answer: Monday Sales CRM offers more visual, no-code customization of pipelines and fields than most dedicated CRMs like Pipedrive, while HubSpot CRM offers a more extensive built-in marketing suite; Monday Sales CRM sits closer to a flexible, board-based CRM than a fully opinionated sales platform.

use_cases.description: Sales teams track deals from lead to close using customizable, visual pipeline boards.

use_cases.title: Post-sale account management
use_cases.description: Account managers use the same CRM to manage onboarding, renewals, and ongoing client projects after a deal closes.

use_cases.title: Lead capture and outreach
use_cases.description: Marketing and sales teams capture leads via forms and run email sequences directly from the CRM.

enrichment.short_description: monday.com is a cloud-based Work OS platform that lets teams build customizable boards, workflows, and apps for project management, operations, and cross-team collaboration.
enrichment.seo_title: monday.com Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: monday.com review 2026: a no-code Work OS for project management and team collaboration. Features, pricing tiers, and top monday.com alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: monday.com is a publicly traded, cloud-based Work Operating System (Work OS) that lets teams build customizable boards, workflows, dashboards, and applications for project management, operations, marketing, and cross-team collaboration without writing code. The company was founded in Tel Aviv, Israel in 2012 by Roy Mann and Eran Zinman under the original name dapulse Labs Ltd, and formally renamed monday.com Ltd in December 2017. monday.com filed for a U.S. IPO in May 2021 and began trading on Nasdaq on June 10, 2021, having raised a total of roughly $235 million across prior funding rounds before going public; the company is headquartered in Tel Aviv with additional offices in New York, London, Sydney, Tokyo, and other major cities, and employs well over 2,000 people globally. Architecturally, monday.com's core building block is the 'board,' a customizable grid of items and columns (status, people, dates, numbers, formulas, and more) that can be visualized as tables, Kanban boards, Gantt/timeline charts, calendars, or dashboards. On top of this foundation, the company has built four distinct standalone products, each tailored to a specific workflow: monday Work Management for general project and task management, monday CRM (Monday Sales CRM) for sales pipeline management, monday dev for software development and agile workflows, and monday service for IT and customer service ticketing. All four products share the same underlying Work OS engine, automation system, and integration marketplace, letting customers mix and match products within one account. Core platform features include no-code automations that trigger actions based on rules, over 200 pre-built templates, native integrations with tools like Slack, Gmail, Outlook, Zoom, and Jira, AI-assisted features for summarizing updates and generating content, time tracking, Gantt and timeline views, and granular permissions for private boards and guest access. monday.com's pricing follows a freemium, seat-based subscription model with five tiers: Free (up to two seats, limited boards), Basic (around $9 per seat per month annually), Standard (around $12), Pro (around $19), and Enterprise (custom pricing with advanced security, governance, and higher automation/API limits); seats are sold in blocks starting at three and scaling in increments of five, and monthly billing costs more per seat than annual billing. monday.com's target audience spans small teams up to large enterprises across virtually every industry, given its horizontal, highly configurable design. Its main competitors include Asana, ClickUp, Smartsheet, Airtable, Trello (Atlassian), and Wrike. monday.com differentiates itself through its highly visual, no-code customization, the breadth of its multi-product Work OS suite covering project management, CRM, dev, and service use cases from one platform, and its public-company scale and integration ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: monday.com is a cloud-based Work Operating System that lets teams build fully customizable boards, dashboards, and automated workflows without writing code, spanning project management, operations, marketing, and cross-team collaboration. Its flexible board-and-column architecture can be reshaped into tables, Kanban boards, Gantt timelines, or calendars depending on the team's needs.

The company was founded in Tel Aviv in 2012 by Roy Mann and Eran Zinman as dapulse Labs, rebranded to monday.com in 2017, and went public on Nasdaq in June 2021 after raising roughly $235 million in prior funding. It now employs well over 2,000 people across offices in Tel Aviv, New York, London, and other cities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: monday.com's platform is organized around four standalone products built on a shared Work OS foundation: monday Work Management for general project tracking, monday CRM for sales, monday dev for software teams, and monday service for IT/customer service. All four share the same automation engine, integrations, and dashboard tools.

Core capabilities include no-code automations, over 200 pre-built templates, native integrations with tools like Slack, Gmail, and Jira, AI features for summarizing updates and generating content, time tracking, Gantt/timeline views, and granular permissions including private boards and guest access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: monday.com uses a freemium, seat-based subscription model with five tiers: Free (up to two seats), Basic (around $9 per seat per month annually), Standard (around $12), Pro (around $19), and custom-priced Enterprise. Seats are sold in blocks, starting at a minimum of three and scaling in increments of five.

Annual billing offers a meaningfully lower per-seat rate than monthly billing, and higher tiers unlock more automation and API call allowances, advanced views like Gantt and timeline, and enterprise-grade security and governance controls.

features.title: Customizable boards
features.description: Flexible grid-based boards with configurable columns that can be visualized as tables, Kanban, timelines, or calendars.

features.title: No-code automations
features.description: Rule-based automations that trigger actions like notifications, status changes, and task assignments without coding.

features.title: Multi-product Work OS suite
features.description: Four standalone products (Work Management, CRM, dev, service) built on one shared platform and account.

features.title: Dashboards and reporting
features.description: Customizable dashboards that aggregate data across boards for high-level reporting and visibility.

features.title: Native integrations
features.description: Pre-built integrations with tools like Slack, Gmail, Outlook, Zoom, and Jira to connect existing workflows.

features.title: AI-assisted features
features.description: AI tools for summarizing updates, generating content, and assisting with task management.

features.title: Time tracking and Gantt views
features.description: Built-in time tracking plus Gantt and timeline views for scheduling and resource planning.

features.title: Templates library
features.description: Over 200 pre-built templates across departments and use cases to accelerate setup.

pros: Highly visual and flexible interface that adapts to many different workflows
Strong no-code automation and integration ecosystem
Single platform spans project management, CRM, dev, and service use cases
Large template library shortens setup time for new teams
Public company with strong financial backing and frequent feature investment
cons: Pricing scales quickly for larger teams, especially on Pro and Enterprise tiers
Minimum three-seat requirement and 5-seat increments can be awkward for small teams
Advanced features like time tracking and Gantt views are locked to higher tiers
Can become complex to administer at scale with many boards and automations
Some core reporting and automation limits require moving up a paid tier

faqs.question: What is monday.com used for?
faqs.answer: monday.com is used as a general-purpose Work OS for project management, task tracking, operations, marketing workflows, and cross-team collaboration, using customizable no-code boards and automations.

faqs.question: How much does monday.com cost?
faqs.answer: monday.com offers a free plan for up to two seats, with paid plans starting around $9 per seat per month for Basic (billed annually), scaling to Standard, Pro, and custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Who founded monday.com?
faqs.answer: monday.com was founded in Tel Aviv in 2012 by Roy Mann and Eran Zinman, originally under the name dapulse, before rebranding to monday.com in 2017.

faqs.question: Is monday.com publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes, monday.com Ltd went public on Nasdaq on June 10, 2021, after filing for its IPO in May 2021.

faqs.question: What is the difference between monday.com and Monday Sales CRM?
faqs.answer: monday.com is the general-purpose Work OS platform for project and work management, while Monday Sales CRM is a specific, pre-configured product built on the same platform and priced separately, focused on sales pipeline management.

faqs.question: How does monday.com compare to Asana or ClickUp?
faqs.answer: monday.com, Asana, and ClickUp are all work management platforms; monday.com is known for its highly visual, spreadsheet-like board customization and its multi-product Work OS suite spanning CRM, dev, and service use cases beyond core project management.

use_cases.title: Cross-team project management
use_cases.description: Teams plan, track, and report on projects using customizable boards, timelines, and dashboards.

use_cases.title: Marketing campaign planning
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use monday.com boards to plan content calendars, campaigns, and creative workflows.

use_cases.title: Enterprise workflow standardization
use_cases.description: Large organizations standardize processes across departments using monday.com's Work OS and its Work Management, CRM, dev, and service products together.
